questions.question: Explain the difference between a system call and a library function. Give one example of each.
keyPoints: System call crosses user-kernel boundary with privilege transition
Library function executes entirely in user space
Example system call: open(), read(), write(), fork()
Example library function: fopen(), fread(), printf(), strcpy()
C library wraps system calls for portability and error handling
System calls return error codes via errno; library functions may return NULL or set error indicators

questions.question: What is the role of the Unix kernel in system programming? How does a user program communicate with the kernel?
keyPoints: Kernel manages hardware resources and provides core OS services
Handles process scheduling, memory management, file systems, I/O, IPC
User programs communicate via system calls (software interrupts/traps)
C library provides wrapper functions around system calls
Examples: open(), read(), write(), fork(), exec(), wait()

questions.question: Describe the Unix philosophy and explain how it influences system program design.
keyPoints: Write programs that do one thing and do it well
Programs should work together (composability)
Use text streams as universal interface
Small is beautiful - simple, modular design
Programs should be easily replaceable for improvement
Encourages pipelines and filters architecture

questions.question: What is POSIX and why is it important for Unix System Programming?
keyPoints: POSIX: Portable Operating System Interface - IEEE standard
Defines API, command-line interface, and shell utilities
Ensures source code portability across POSIX-compliant systems
Major standards: POSIX.1 (API), POSIX.2 (shell/commands), POSIX.4 (threads)
Reduces need for conditional compilation with #ifdef guards
Examples: unistd.h, pthread.h provide POSIX interfaces

questions.question: Explain the concept of file descriptors in Unix. What are the standard file descriptors 0, 1, and 2?
keyPoints: File descriptor is a small non-negative integer
Represents an entry in per-process file descriptor table
Used by system calls: read(), write(), close(), dup()
FD 0 = Standard Input (stdin) - by convention, connected to terminal
FD 1 = Standard Output (stdout) - by convention, terminal output
FD 2 = Standard Error (stderr) - by convention, error output
File descriptors can be redirected using dup2() or shell operators <, >, 2>

questions.question: What is the purpose of errno in Unix system programming? How should a programmer handle errors from system calls?
keyPoints: errno is a global variable set by C library on system call failure
Holds error code indicating specific failure reason
Common codes: EACCES (permission), ENOENT (not found), EBADF (bad fd)
Check return value first (typically -1 indicates error)
Then check errno for diagnostic information
Use perror() or strerror() to print human-readable messages
Always check error returns for robust programs

questions.question: Describe the process lifecycle in Unix from creation to termination.
keyPoints: Process created via fork() - creates copy of parent
Child may call exec() to replace its memory with new program
Both parent and child execute concurrently
Process can wait for child using wait() or waitpid()
Process terminates via exit() or receiving signal
Parent receives exit status via wait() system call
Kernel reclaims process resources (memory, file descriptors, PID)

questions.question: Explain the organization of Unix system programming covering user space, kernel space, system calls, and the C library. Include a diagram in your answer.
keyPoints: Diagram showing: Application → C Library → System Call Interface → Kernel → Hardware
User Space: Where user applications run with limited privileges
Kernel Space: Privileged mode with full hardware access
System Calls: Interface mechanism transitioning from user to kernel mode
C Library (libc): Provides wrappers around system calls for portability
Kernel responsibilities: process management, memory, file I/O, device communication, security
System call numbers map to specific kernel functions
Error handling via return values and errno
Multiple processes share CPU via scheduling; kernel manages time-slicing

questions.question: Discuss the various categories of Unix system calls with examples of each. Explain how system calls enable user programs to request kernel services securely.
keyPoints: Process Control: fork(), exec(), wait(), exit(), getpid()
File Management: open(), read(), write(), close(), lseek()
Device Communication: ioctl(), read(), write() on device files
Information Maintenance: getuid(), uname(), getpid(), time()
Communication: pipe(), socket(), msgget(), semget()
Protection: chmod(), chowner(), setuid(), umask()
Security: Kernel validates all requests, checks permissions
System calls use trap instruction to switch to kernel mode
Each system call has a number used as index in system call table
Arguments passed via registers; kernel validates before execution
Return value indicates success (0 or positive) or failure (-1)

questions.question: What are the advantages of Unix system programming using C? Discuss portability considerations and the role of standards like POSIX.
keyPoints: C provides low-level memory access via pointers without runtime overhead
Direct access to system calls mirrors Unix kernel design (written in C)
Minimal runtime library needed - suitable for embedded systems
Compiled code is machine-independent at source level
Standards like POSIX ensure source portability across Unix variants
POSIX defines: API functions, behavior, error conditions, constants
Header files: <unistd.h>, <sys/types.h>, <fcntl.h> for POSIX compliance
Use #ifdef for non-portable features to maintain portability
System V vs BSD differences abstracted by POSIX
Portable programs avoid implementation-defined behaviors
Test on multiple platforms or use abstraction layers for full portability
